blocks.html:
This section exists because the other fifteen assume a vocabulary they never teach. Work through it once and the rest of the course becomes readable. It covers two things: the big picture of who talks to whom, and the notation — how to read JSON and code snippets on sight.

Module 1: The big picture — one round trip

Every interaction with Claude is the same round trip:

  1. Your app builds a request: the system prompt + the tool definitions + the whole conversation so far.
  2. It sends that request to an endpoint — an address, like a mailing address for a service. api.anthropic.com/v1/messages is the address for “talk to Claude.” An endpoint is not where data is stored; it is where requests are sent.
  3. Anthropic's server runs the model and sends back a response — Claude's answer.
  4. Then the server forgets everything. That is what stateless means. Not time — memory. Every request arrives as if it were the first one ever. If you want Claude to “remember,” your app must store the history and re-send it in the next request.

Inside a request, settings travel as parameters — named fields, like fields on a form. tool_choice: "auto" means: the parameter named tool_choice is set to the value auto. When a flashcard names a parameter in backticks, it is pointing at one specific field on that form.

Hold onto this map. Statelessness, caching, context windows, and prompt design all become obvious consequences of this one round trip.

Module 2: How to read JSON. JSON is not code — it is data written down as text, in a strict fill-in-the-form format. There are only three shapes to learn:

  • Object — curly braces, holding labeled values: {"name": "Tony", "age": 47}. Read it as “an object where name is Tony and age is 47.” Each entry is "label": value, comma-separated.
  • List — square brackets, holding items in order: ["red", "green", "blue"]. Read it as “a list of three colors.”
  • Values — text goes in quotes ("Chicago"), numbers don't (1280.5), and true/false are yes/no switches.

They nest. Practice on this real one:

{
  "customer": "Acme",
  "items": ["widget", "gadget"],
  "paid": false
}

Out loud: “An object: customer is Acme, items is a list of widget and gadget, paid is no.” That skill — reading braces out loud — is 80% of JSON literacy, and every section of this course assumes it.

Module 3: How to read code snippets. The flashcards use a compact notation that looks like programming but follows just four rules:

  • get_order(order_id: 123) — read as “run the function named get_order, giving it order_id set to 123.” The pattern is always name(input: value).
  • snake_case — underscores glue multi-word names together. get_order is just “get order.” Programmers can't use spaces in names, so underscores stand in.
  • Backticks / code font — when a card writes dry_run: boolean, the code font means “this exact literal name in the system,” not a general concept. boolean means the value is true/false.
  • “Returns” — what a function hands back when it finishes. “The tool returns an empty list” = the function ran fine and the answer was: nothing found.

So “the tool search_flights(destination: "ORD") returned []” decodes to: the search-flights function was run with destination set to ORD, and it came back with an empty list — no flights found. Not an error. That distinction is a real exam question.

Module 4: The tool call handshake

A tool is a function your app offers to the model. Claude cannot run anything itself — it can only ask. The handshake is always the same four steps:

  1. Claude outputs a structured request — “please run get_order with order_id: 123” — then stops and waits.
  2. Your code executes the function.
  3. Your code sends the result back in the next request.
  4. Claude reads the result and continues.

Four terms chain together around this handshake, and they are one connected story:

  • Schema — the rulebook declaring what shape data must have (“an object with name as text and age as a number”).
  • Validation — the check against that rulebook. “Failed validation” means the data arrived but broke a rule.
  • Error handling — the “if this fails, then what?” plan you write in advance: retry, tell the user, fall back, or stop gracefully.
  • Retry — the most common error-handling move: attempt again, usually after a short wait.

Schema defines the rules, validation enforces them, error handling decides what happens on failure, retry is one of its options. When a question says “the extraction failed validation — what goes in the correction request?”, it is asking about step 3 of this chain.

These are the traps this section was built from — each row is a misreading that will cost you exam points if it survives:

TermCommon misreadingWhat it actually means
Stateless“Something about time”The server keeps no memory between requests; your app re-sends everything each turn
Request/responseTool talking to toolYour app ↔ Anthropic's server, one round trip
EndpointWhere data is storedThe address you send requests to
Prompt cachingClearing context to save tokensServer reuses your unchanged prefix at ~10% price; a cost lever, not a memory feature
PrefillStored info auto-added when neededYou write the start of Claude's answer; it must continue from there
Prompt injectionMalicious code infecting the systemPlain words in content that the model may obey as commands — no code involved
RAGA special databaseA recipe: search a knowledge store, paste top results into the prompt, then answer
AgentAnything that senses and respondsAn LLM in a loop with tools: act, observe result, decide next step, repeat
ValidationTesting that a connection worksChecking data against schema rules before trusting it
429Something brokeRate limit — “slow down and retry”; nothing is broken

items: One round trip: your app sends system prompt + tools + conversation to an endpoint; the server responds, then forgets everything (stateless = no memory, not time).
JSON has three shapes: {objects} with labeled values, [lists] of items, and plain values. Read braces out loud until it's automatic.
name(input: value) means “run the function called name with these inputs” — and snake_case is just words glued with underscores.
Claude never executes tools. It asks, stops, and waits; your code runs the function and reports back.
Schema → validation → error handling → retry is one chain: the rulebook, the check, the failure plan, and the most common recovery move.
